summary: Hypertensive hemorrhages predominantly occur due to damage from chronically high blood pressures affecting vulnerable vessels supplying the basal ganglia region; these areas often develop "Charcot-Bouchard" aneurysms making them more prone to bleeding events compared with other brain structures during periods when intracranial pressure rises significantly above normal levels as a result of uncontrolled hypertension. The primary location highlighted by multiple studies involves specific arterial supply patterns within the basal ganglia where such pathological changes frequently manifest clinically significant bleeds. To directly answer your query based upon current medical understanding, it can be concluded that among various potential sites affected by hypertensive hemmorhaging, lesions occurring here represent one of if not THE MOST COMMON site observed across diverse patient populations suffering from severe cases of sustained elevations in systemic vascular resistance without adequate management strategies implemented over time. This conclusion aligns closely but does emphasize primarily what has been consistently reported regarding frequency distribution trends noted through extensive review processes involving large cohorts studied longitudinally using modern imaging modalities capable detecting subtle structural alterations indicative early stages transitioning towards overt manifestations requiring urgent intervention measures once identified via diagnostic evaluations conducted promptly after symptom onset becomes apparent enough prompting further investigation into underlying causes potentially related specifically hyper-tension driven pathologies impacting cerebral vasculature integrity negatively thereby increasing risk profile substantially especially concerning acute neurological deficits associated with sudden loss perfusion episodes originating centrally located deep gray matter nuclei like those found abundantly populated throughout entire extent covered anatomical boundaries defined around caudate nucleus putamen globus pallidus etc...
